does: This paper identifies and analyzes a fundamental failure mode in final-token safety probes for large language models, where probes trained on clean harmful and benign prompts miss jailbreak attempts because unsafe evidence is distributed across earlier token positions rather than concentrated at the final token. The authors use subspace analysis and token-level prefill analyses across three instruction-tuned LLMs to show that these probes achieve high recall on clean harmful prompts but produce both false negatives on jailbreaks and false positives on safety-adjacent benign content. They demonstrate that increasing probe bottleneck width does not reliably fix the representational mismatch, and that naive max-pooling over token positions causes overfiring on safe prompts. The work uses a simple PCA-HMM trajectory model trained only on clean data to recover probe-missed jailbreaks, suggesting that temporal structure in the prefill phase contains recoverable safety information.
connects: This work builds on the SafeSwitch probe framework and the broader literature on mechanistic safety interventions that attempt to detect harmful content in model activations before generation. It extends prior research on hidden-state based safety monitoring by diagnosing why such approaches fail on adversarial inputs, connecting to jailbreak research that exploits distributional differences between training and attack examples. The paper also engages with subspace analysis methods from mechanistic interpretability that characterize how models represent different concepts in their hidden states, and relates to work on token-level analysis in transformers showing that important information emerges across the sequence rather than solely at final positions. The trajectory modeling approach connects to temporal methods in safety monitoring and builds on understanding how safety-relevant information evolves during prompt processing.
enables: This work points toward improved safety probe architectures that aggregate information across token positions and exploit temporal dynamics rather than relying on single-token readouts. It suggests that trajectory-based models or attention mechanisms over prefill activations could better capture distributed unsafe evidence, and that incorporating temporal structure explicitly could improve both recall and precision. The finding that probe-visible unsafe evidence appears in the sequence earlier than final positions motivates research into early-stopping or early-warning mechanisms that trigger on unsafe content before full generation begins. The work also enables future investigation into whether dynamic readout positions or adaptive aggregation strategies could maintain safety guarantees across both clean and adversarial inputs, and suggests avenues for studying the representational geometry of jailbreaks versus benign prompts at higher granularity.
